did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34 architectures, to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) skin cancer from dermoscopic images. The study achieved classification accuracy of 98.9% for EfficientNet-B0 and 100% for ResNet-34, integrating these deep learning models into an Edge AI and Streamlit web application for real-time diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a Basal Cell Carcinoma classification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) integrated with Edge AI and Streamlit. It details specific methodologies including dataset collection (USK-Normal Skin and ISIC 2018), preprocessing, model training, and performance evaluation (accuracy, recall, specificity, F-score, inference speed). The study reports new results, such as 98.9% and 100% accuracy for the respective models, and describes the deployment on a Jetson Orin Nano device. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE ICONS-IoT), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integration of Edge AI and Streamlit Web Application for Basal Cell Carcinoma Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Networking, Intelligent Systems, and IoT (ICONS-IoT)
brief_summary: This study developed a Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) classification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) integrated with Edge AI and a Streamlit web interface. The models were trained on a dataset comprising 3,000 normal skin images from the USK-Normal Skin dataset and 3,000 BCC images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, recall, specificity, F-score, and inference speed, with ResNet-34 achieving 100% accuracy and EfficientNet-B0 achieving 98.9%. The system was deployed on a Jetson Orin Nano edge device, demonstrating that EfficientNet-B0 offered superior inference efficiency compared to ResNet-34. The application enables real-time, local diagnosis without requiring an internet connection, thereby preserving patient privacy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary disease class studied. The paper focuses on classifying BCC skin cancer using images from the ISIC 2018 dataset (3,000 images).
The negative class used for binary classification. The paper used 3,000 normal skin images from the USK-Normal Skin dataset as the comparator to BCC.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Normal Skin
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between two discrete diagnostic categories: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Normal Skin. The models (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) predict class labels, with performance evaluated using accuracy, recall, specificity, and F-score. The confusion matrix shows True Positive, False Positive, False Negative, and True Negative counts for these two classes.

datasets_used.name: USK-Normal Skin dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing normal skin images used as the negative class for binary classification. Accessed via https://ademift.github.io/USK-NORMAL-SKIN-DATASET/.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) as the negative class.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance on normal skin images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset containing skin disease images. The study specifically utilized the Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) subset from this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) as the positive class.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance on BCC images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

USK-Normal Skin and ISIC 2018 (Combined.metric: Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, F-Score, Training Time, Inference Time
USK-Normal Skin and ISIC 2018 (Combined.notes: Both models achieved perfect recall (100%), meaning all BCC cases were correctly identified. ResNet-34 achieved 100% specificity, while EfficientNet-B0 achieved 97.8% specificity. EfficientNet-B0 demonstrated superior inference efficiency compared to ResNet-34 on the Jetson Orin Nano edge device. ResNet-34 showed higher validation loss fluctuations compared to EfficientNet-B0, suggesting potential overfitting or less stable generalization despite high accuracy.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (models chosen for effectiveness as optimized CNNs with small parameter counts suitable for edge devices)
architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported (described as having a 'relatively small number of parameters')

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping: Original images (600x450 pixels) were cropped to 450x450 pixels to obtain a square shape.
Resizing: Cropped images were resized from 450x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels to match the input requirements of the CNN architectures.
Data Splitting: The dataset was split into training (70%), testing (20%), and validation (10%) sets using stratified sampling.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper explicitly states that data augmentation was not applied during the training process.

conclusions: The study successfully developed a Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) classification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-34) integrated with Edge AI and a Streamlit web interface.

ResNet-34 achieved 100% accuracy, recall, specificity, and F-score, while EfficientNet-B0 achieved 98.9% accuracy, 100% recall, 97.8% specificity, and 98.9% F-score on the combined dataset of 3,000 normal skin images and 3,000 BCC images.

EfficientNet-B0 demonstrated superior inference efficiency compared to ResNet-34 on the Jetson Orin Nano edge device, with significantly faster inference times for batch sizes of 50 (2.9s vs 5.96s) and 100 (10.66s vs 26.93s).

The integration of Edge AI allows for local data processing on the device, which reduces dependence on internet connections, enables faster classification, and preserves patient privacy by avoiding external data transmission.
ResNet-34 showed higher validation loss fluctuations compared to EfficientNet-B0, suggesting potential overfitting or less stable generalization despite its perfect accuracy metrics.
The system enables real-time, local diagnosis accessible to doctors and general users via a web interface without requiring complex software installations or an internet connection.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Normal Skin), excluding other critical skin cancer types such as Melanoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma, which limits its clinical utility for comprehensive skin cancer screening.

No data augmentation was applied during training, which may contribute to the observed overfitting in the ResNet-34 model, evidenced by high validation loss fluctuations despite achieving 100% accuracy on the test set.

The dataset consists of only 6,000 images (3,000 BCC from ISIC 2018 and 3,000 Normal Skin from USK-Normal Skin), which is a relatively small sample size for deep learning, potentially limiting the generalizability of the results.

The ResNet-34 model achieved 100% accuracy, recall, specificity, and F-score, which is unusually high for medical image classification and raises concerns about potential data leakage or an overly simple classification boundary given the lack of data augmentation.

The EfficientNet-B0 model showed a specificity of 97.8% (21.7% False Positive rate in the confusion matrix normalization), indicating a notable risk of misdiagnosing healthy skin as cancerous, which could lead to unnecessary anxiety and procedures.

The study lacks external validation on independent datasets or diverse demographic populations, relying solely on the provided public datasets, which limits the assessment of real-world robustness and bias.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance of the two architectures, making it difficult to determine if the differences in inference speed and accuracy are statistically significant.
The system was deployed on a specific edge device (Jetson Orin Nano); performance and efficiency may vary significantly on other hardware platforms with different computational capabilities.

The paper acknowledges potential overfitting in ResNet-34 and suggests k-fold cross-validation as a future improvement, indicating that the current evaluation methodology may not fully capture model stability.
